t: DSM-5-TR / ICD-11 — in the anxiety disorders category; 'selective' — not voluntary choice, but situation-specific anxiety response.
Home video recordings of speech — diagnostic confirmation.
Comorbid anxiety, autism, language disorder screening.
Language is poor in all contexts.
During adaptation to a new language — typically months.
Comorbid severe — patient speaks but fears social evaluation.
SMQ — gold standard; SAS (School Anxiety Scale).
— graded exposure, fading-in technique (gradual introduction of the individual to the environment).
Integrated Behavioral Therapy (Bergman R.L.) — manualized, with family + school involvement.
School-based intervention — gradual increase in communication demands, teacher coordination.
SSRI (fluoxetine, sertraline) – in moderate to severe or non-responsive to behavioral intervention cases. Black B. et al. JAACAP 1994 RCT.
Reducing accommodation — stopping the parent from speaking for the child.
AACAP — behavioral intervention first-line, SSRI adjunct.
NICE — principles of anxiety disorders apply.
The patient converses with a comfortable person; gradually a stranger is introduced into this context; then the patient transitions to speaking with a stranger.
Staged exposure, positive reinforcement, systematic desensitization; 20 sessions. Evidence: Bergman R.L. et al. JAACAP 2013 RCT.
Evidence base in pediatric selective mutism; Black B., Uhde T.W. JAACAP 1994 RCT.
Speech recovers in most children with early intervention.
Without treatment, chronicity and transition to social anxiety disorder.
selective mutism is an anxiety disorder — the child CANNOT speak, the position 'does not want to speak' is incorrect.
Myth 2: "If a child does not speak, strong pressure is needed"
pressure intensifies anxiety and mutism; stepped exposure recommended.
Myth 3: "It will resolve with age; no intervention is needed"
Without early intervention, academic and social impairment persists; risk of transition to social anxiety disorder in adulthood.
Myth 4: "Mutism in a bilingual child is normal"
"Silent period" — normal for a few months when learning a new language; mutism lasting ≥ 6 months or occurring in both languages — consider selective mutism diagnosis.
Myth 5: "Only deep psychoanalytic therapy is effective"
Behavior-based and CBT approaches are first-line; psychoanalytic intervention lacks evidence base.
Bergman R.L. et al. Prevalence and description of selective mutism in a school-based sample. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 2002;41(8):938–946.
Bergman R.L., Gonzalez A., Piacentini J., Keller M.L. Integrated Behavior Therapy for Selective Mutism: a randomized controlled pilot study. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 2013;52(7):680–689.
Black B., Uhde T.W. Treatment of elective mutism with fluoxetine: a double-blind, placebo-controlled study. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 1994;33(7):1000–1006.
